disease.name: Acute Appendicitis
disease.note: Classic migration of pain from periumbilical to RLQ is the hallmark — but present in only 50-60% of cases. Atypical in young children (<5 yrs), pregnant women, and the elderly. In our setting, late presentation with perforation is common due to use of local painkillers and traditional remedies before seeking care. Retrocaecal appendix can masquerade as right loin pain or even renal colic.
presentingComplaints: Pain around the belly button moving to the right side
Pain on the right lower side of the abdomen
Unable to walk straight due to pain

history_questions.question: When did the pain start?
Did the pain start around your belly button and move to the right side?
Is the pain constant or does it come and go?
Have you lost your appetite?
Did you vomit? Did the pain start before or after the vomiting?
Have you had fever or chills?
Does the pain get worse when you cough, sneeze, or move?
Have you taken any painkillers before coming to hospital?
Have you used any local herbs or traditional medicines?
When did you last pass stool and gas?
Have you had similar pain before?
Do you have any medical conditions like diabetes, high blood pressure, or HIV?
For women: Could you be pregnant? When was your last menstrual period?
Do you have pain passing urine or blood in urine?
Have you had any previous abdominal surgeries?
What did you eat in the last 24 hours? Have you eaten ugali, omena, or any street food?
Is anyone else in your home sick with similar symptoms?
Do you feel the pain in your back or your right flank?
Have you had diarrhea or constipation recently?
Are you currently on any antibiotics or other medications?
In children: Have they been irritable, refusing to feed, or crying when you pick them up?
In the elderly: Have you felt generally weak, confused, or had a fall recently?
In pregnant women: Is the pain higher up or on the right side? Pregnancy can shift the appendix upward.
Do you have a cough or chest pain? (to rule out pneumonia as a mimic)
Have you travelled recently to a different region or country?
Do you have HIV? If yes, what is your CD4 count and are you on ARVs?
Have you had TB in the past or been exposed to someone with TB?
What work do you do? (boda-boda driver, farmer, market vendor — affects follow-up and time to seek care)
How far did you travel to reach this hospital? How long were you in pain before deciding to come?
Have you consulted a traditional healer or taken herbal remedies before coming?
Do you have any known allergies (especially to antibiotics like penicillin)?
For children: Have they had recent sore throat, cold, or viral illness? (mesenteric adenitis mimic)
Do you have a family history of appendicitis?
Have you noticed any skin rash or joint pains? (suggests Henoch-Schonlein purpura in children)
Do you have any abdominal pain that wakes you from sleep?
Have you had any weight loss recently?
Do you experience pain when you pass stool?
Are you a smoker? Does someone in your home smoke?
Have you ever been tested for sickle cell disease or trait?
Do you have any history of hernia in your groin?

examination_findings.finding: RLQ tenderness at McBurney's point
Rebound tenderness in RLQ
Guarding in right lower quadrant
Rovsing's sign (pressure on LLQ causes RLQ pain)
Psoas sign (pain on right hip extension)
Obturator sign (pain on internal rotation of flexed right hip)
Low-grade fever (37.5-38.5°C)
Tachycardia
Generalized rigidity (suggests perforation)
Diffuse abdominal tenderness (suggests perforation)
Absent bowel sounds (ileus)
Pain on coughing (cough sign)
Dunphy's sign (pain on coughing localized to RLQ)
Cutaneous hyperesthesia over right T10-T12 dermatome
Foul-smelling breath and coated tongue (late, gangrenous)
Right-sided pelvic tenderness on digital rectal exam in male or PV exam in female
Dehydration signs (dry mucosa, sunken eyes, reduced skin turgor)
Markle's sign (jar tenderness — heel-drop jarring causes RLQ pain)
Baldwin's sign (flexion of right thigh at hip may be maintained in retrocecal appendicitis)
Ten Horn's sign (gentle traction of right spermatic cord causes pain in appendicitis)
Bastedo's sign (distension of the colon by air insufflation causes RLQ pain)
Soresi's sign (patient lifts right leg against resistance: pain in RLQ)
Aure-Rosano's sign (hyperesthesia over the right iliac crest)
Abdominal wall rigidity with guarding on the right side only
In children: lying still, irritable when moved, drawing up of right leg
In pregnant women: tenderness may be in RUQ or right flank due to cephalad displacement of appendix
In the elderly: decreased tenderness despite advanced pathology — high index of suspicion needed
Palpable RLQ mass (suggests appendiceal phlegmon or abscess)
Mucosal pallor and conjunctival pallor (if prolonged illness with haemolysis)
Livedo reticularis or mottled extremities (late sepsis)

interpretation.low: Score 0-4: Unlikely appendicitis. Observe or investigate further.
interpretation.moderate: Score 5-6: Possible appendicitis. Consider imaging (US or CT).
interpretation.high: Score 7-10: Probable appendicitis. Strongly consider appendicectomy.

interpretation.low: Score 0-4: Low probability. Observe or discharge with safety netting.
interpretation.moderate: Score 5-8: Indeterminate. Admit for observation ± imaging.
interpretation.high: Score 9-12: High probability. Proceed to appendicectomy.

riskFactors.note: Peak incidence in adolescents and young adults
Rare under 5 years but higher perforation rate
Fecalith, lymphoid hyperplasia, foreign body, parasites
Ascaris lumbricoides, Enterobius vermicularis can obstruct appendiceal lumen

subtypes.name: Simple (Non-Perforated/Catarrhal) Appendicitis
subtypes.management: Emergency appendicectomy (open or laparoscopic). Single-dose prophylactic antibiotics (Ceftriaxone 2 g IV + Metronidazole 500 mg IV). Early oral intake post-op. Discharge within 24-48 hours. Antibiotic-only therapy is an option in selected patients who decline surgery, with success rates of 60-80% at 1 year but 20-40% recurrence.

subtypes.name: Perforated (Gangrenous) Appendicitis
subtypes.management: Emergency laparotomy or laparoscopy. Generous peritoneal lavage with warm saline (3-5 litres). IV antibiotics for 5-7 days (Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole + Gentamicin). ICU admission if septic. Delayed primary closure or leave wound open if grossly contaminated.

subtypes.name: Appendiceal Mass / Phlegmon
subtypes.management: Non-operative management: IV antibiotics (Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole), NBM, IV fluids. Monitor mass size. Interval appendicectomy in 6-12 weeks if symptoms resolve. Emergency surgery if mass enlarges or peritonitis develops.

subtypes.name: Retrocecal Appendicitis
subtypes.management: Appendicectomy. May require mobilization of cecum to access retrocecal appendix. Higher risk of delayed diagnosis and perforation. Watch for psoas abscess formation.

subtypes.name: Pelvic Appendicitis
subtypes.management: Appendicectomy (laparoscopic preferred for better visualization of pelvic appendix). Be aware of proximity to right ureter and iliac vessels. May present with urinary symptoms mimicking UTI.

subtypes.name: Subhepatic Appendicitis (Malrotation / High Appendix)
subtypes.management: Laparoscopic appendicectomy — identifies ectopic position. Must differentiate from cholecystitis. If malrotation found, assess for Ladd's bands. Higher index of suspicion when RLQ signs are absent.

mimics.whyMimic: Causes RLQ pain, fever, leukocytosis often in children and young adults
Sudden RLQ pain, nausea, vomiting in women of reproductive age
RLQ pain radiating to groin, nausea, vomiting
Can present with right-sided pain if gallbladder is low-lying or with atypical radiation
RLQ pain, fever, tenderness that mimics appendicitis clinically
RLQ pain, peritonism, vomiting in women of childbearing age
RLQ pain, fever, diarrhea in immunocompromised patients
RLQ referred pain, fever, leukocytosis, especially in children
Abdominal pain, vomiting, fever in children — can mimic appendicitis before rash appears
Acute RLQ or LLQ pain, tenderness, and localized peritonism
RLQ/loin pain, nausea, vomiting, may have tenderness

bedside.name: Alvarado Score (MANTRELS)
bedside.indication: All suspected appendicitis cases for risk stratification

bedside.name: Digital Rectal Examination
bedside.indication: Right-sided tenderness suggests pelvic appendix. Exclude prostatic pathology in men

bedside.name: Pregnancy test (urine)
bedside.indication: All women of reproductive age — mandatory before surgery

laboratory.name: WBC count
laboratory.indication: Leukocytosis >10,000/mm³ supports diagnosis. >15,000 suggests perforation. ALWAYS interpret with clinical picture — WBC can be normal in up to 20% of cases.

laboratory.name: CRP
laboratory.indication: CRP >10 mg/L supports inflammation. >50 mg/L suggests advanced pathology. CRP peaks at 24-48 hours.

laboratory.name: Procalcitonin
laboratory.indication: Elevated in perforated appendicitis. Helps distinguish simple from complicated.

laboratory.name: Lactate
laboratory.indication: Lactate >2 mmol/L suggests ischemia/gangrene. >4 suggests perforation with sepsis.

laboratory.name: Urinalysis
laboratory.indication: Rule out UTI, pyelonephritis, ureteric stones. Trace protein/leukocytes may be in appendicitis due to proximity to ureter.

laboratory.name: Urea and Creatinine
laboratory.indication: Dehydration assessment. Pre-operative baseline for anesthesia.

laboratory.name: Beta-hCG (serum)
laboratory.indication: If urine pregnancy test equivocal. Rule out ectopic pregnancy.

laboratory.name: HIV test
laboratory.indication: High prevalence setting. Atypical presentation more likely in HIV.

imaging.name: Abdominal Ultrasound
imaging.indication: First-line imaging. Visualize appendix diameter >6mm, non-compressible, wall thickness >3mm, periappendiceal fluid, appendicolith. Graded compression technique essential. Operator-dependent.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen with IV contrast
imaging.indication: Gold standard for equivocal cases, suspected perforation, atypical presentation, elderly, or obese. Show dilated appendix >7mm, wall enhancement, periappendiceal fat stranding, abscess, phlegmon.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen without contrast
imaging.indication: Alternative when IV contrast contraindicated (renal failure, allergy)

imaging.name: Chest X-Ray
imaging.indication: Exclude right lower lobe pneumonia as mimic of appendicitis

imaging.name: Abdominal X-Ray supine + erect
imaging.indication: May show appendicolith (10-15%), free air if perforated. Right lumbar scoliosis due to psoas spasm. Not diagnostic but useful if CT unavailable.

imaging.name: Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)
imaging.indication: Pregnant patients with equivocal ultrasound. High sensitivity without radiation.

management.protocolReference: WSES Jerusalem Guidelines for Acute Appendicitis (2020) / EAST Practice Guidelines

resuscitation: NBM (nil by mouth) — prepare for possible surgery
IV fluids — Normal Saline or Ringer's Lactate 1-2 L bolus if dehydrated, then 125 mL/hr maintenance
IV Paracetamol 1 g for pain (avoid masking surgical signs — use judiciously)
Urinary catheter if patient is shocked, elderly, or for monitoring urine output
Type and screen/crossmatch if suspected perforation or haemodynamically unstable
conservative: IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (Ceftriaxone 2 g IV daily + Metronidazole 500 mg IV 8-hourly)
IV analgesia (Paracetamol 1 g 6-hourly ± low-dose Morphine 2.5-5 mg IV PRN)
Serial abdominal examinations (every 2-4 hours by same examiner if possible)
Alvarado score reassessment at 6-12 hours if diagnosis uncertain
CT or US imaging if initial diagnosis equivocal

surgical: Emergency appendicectomy — gold standard for simple and complicated appendicitis
Laparoscopic approach preferred (reduced pain, faster recovery, shorter hospital stay)
Open approach if: laparoscopic unavailable, advanced pregnancy, multiple previous abdominal surgeries, generalized peritonitis with hemodynamic instability
Conversion rate from laparoscopic to open: 5-10% (dense adhesions, gangrenous base, failure to progress)
nonOperative: Antibiotic-only therapy for uncomplicated appendicitis: IV Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole for 24-48h, then oral switch (Ciprofloxacin + Metronidazole or Amoxicillin/Clavulanate) for total 7 days
Success rate 60-80% at 1 year — 20-40% recurrence rate requiring eventual appendicectomy
Patient must consent to heightened observation and accept risk of recurrence
Not recommended for: appendicolith, age >65, immunocompromised, or suspected perforation
Interval appendicectomy at 6-12 weeks for appendiceal mass/phlegmon that resolves

immediate: IV antibiotics: single dose if non-perforated; continue for 5-7 days if perforated (Ceftriaxone 2 g daily + Metronidazole 500 mg 8-hourly ± Gentamicin 5 mg/kg daily)
VTE prophylaxis — SC enoxaparin 40 mg daily (start 6-12 hours post-op)
NG tube removal: at end of surgery or day 1 if output <500 mL
Early mobilization — sit up and dangle legs on day 0, walk on day 1
Pain control: IV Paracetamol 1 g 6-hourly + IV Morphine PRN for breakthrough. Oral switch when tolerating.
Monitor wounds and vital signs 4-hourly for first 24 hours
day1_3: Advance diet: sips → clear fluids → soft diet as tolerated
Remove urinary catheter on day 1 if patient mobile
Wound check: clean and dry. Leave wound covered for 48 hours.
For perforated cases with open wound: start wet-to-dry dressings 8-hourly
Monitor for ileus: listen for bowel sounds, chart NG output if still in situ
Oral analgesia transition: Paracetamol 1 g PO 6-hourly ± Ibuprofen 400 mg PO 8-hourly
Physiotherapy: incentive spirometry, deep breathing exercises
Discharge planning if non-perforated and tolerating diet: target discharge day 1-2
day3_7: Continue IV antibiotics if perforated — monitor WBC, CRP trending down
Wound review daily — remove sutures/staples at day 7-10 (outpatient)
If open wound: continue dressings, assess for secondary closure
Monitor for intra-abdominal abscess: persistent fever, rising WBC despite antibiotics — repeat CT
Diet as tolerated — resume normal diet when bowel function returns
Plan discharge once afebrile, tolerating oral intake, pain controlled on oral analgesia

pathways.criteria: localized RLQ pain, no peritonitis, Alvarado 4-6, symptoms <48 hours, no appendicolith on imaging
pathways.treatment: Laparoscopic appendicectomy. Single-dose preoperative antibiotics (Ceftriaxone 2 g + Metronidazole 500 mg IV). No post-op antibiotics. Discharge day 1-2. May consider antibiotic-only therapy if patient declines surgery.

pathways.criteria: generalized peritonitis, rigidity, septic features, Alvarado 7-10, symptoms >48 hours, WBC >15, CRP >50, free fluid on US/CT
pathways.treatment: Emergency laparotomy or laparoscopy. IV fluids resuscitation. IV antibiotics for 5-7 days (Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole + Gentamicin). ICU admission. Generous peritoneal lavage. Delayed primary closure or wound left open. Post-op antibiotics until afebrile 48h and WBC normalizing. CT-guided drainage if residual abscess.

pathways.criteria: palpable RLQ mass, symptoms >5 days, no generalized peritonitis, stable vitals
pathways.treatment: Non-operative management: IV antibiotics (Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole), NBM, IV fluids. Monitor mass size daily. Interval appendicectomy at 6-12 weeks. If mass enlarges or peritonitis develops → emergency surgery.

laparoscopic_appendicectomy.title: Laparoscopic Appendicectomy (Three-Port Technique)
laparoscopic_appendicectomy.indication: Acute appendicitis (simple, suppurative, or early gangrenous)
laparoscopic_appendicectomy.position: Supine, arms tucked, surgeon on left side of patient
laparoscopic_appendicectomy.incision: Three ports: 10mm umbilical (camera), 5mm suprapubic (working), 5mm left iliac fossa (working)
steps: Patient under general anaesthesia, bladder catheterized, nasogastric tube inserted
Position: supine with left arm tucked, right arm may be abducted if needed
Prepare and drape abdomen from nipples to pubis
Time-out: confirm patient, procedure, consent, antibiotics given
Hasson technique or Veress needle at umbilicus for pneumoperitoneum (12-15 mmHg CO2)
Insert 10mm umbilical port, introduce 30° laparoscope
Inspect peritoneal cavity — note any free fluid, pus, or adhesions
Insert 5mm suprapubic port under direct vision (suprapubic, midline)
Insert 5mm left iliac fossa port under direct vision
Position patient in Trendelenburg with right side up to move small bowel away
Identify appendix: trace taenia coli of cecum to base of appendix
Assess appendix: inflamed, gangrenous, perforated? Note position (retrocecal/pelvic/paracecal)
Create window in mesoappendix near tip using Maryland dissector or hook cautery
Divide mesoappendix: LigaSure / harmonic scalpel / bipolar cautery — secure appendicular artery
Clear appendix base of fat and tissue for 1 cm
Apply two endoloops at base of appendix (PDS or Vicryl endoloops), 5 mm apart
Cut appendix between the endoloops using scissors — leave distal loop on stump
Inspect stump for bleeding or bile leak
Retrieve appendix through 10mm umbilical port (use retrieval bag if inflamed/perforated)
Irrigate RLQ and pelvis with warm saline if perforated or purulent fluid present
Suction all irrigation fluid
Reduce pneumoperitoneum, check for bleeding
Remove ports under direct vision
Close umbilical port fascia with 0 Vicryl (PDS) when port >5mm
Close skin ports with 4-0 Monocryl subcuticular or staples
Apply sterile dressings
keyLandmarks: Taenia coli — leading to appendix base
Right ureter and iliac vessels (retroperitoneal, safe unless retrocecal dissection)

open_appendicectomy.title: Open Appendicectomy (McBurney's Incision)
open_appendicectomy.procedure: Open appendicectomy via right iliac fossa gridiron incision
open_appendicectomy.indication: Acute appendicitis — especially when laparoscopic unavailable, in advanced pregnancy, or generalized peritonitis
open_appendicectomy.incision: Gridiron (McBurney) incision — 4-6 cm oblique incision at McBurney's point. Midline laparotomy if perforated with generalized peritonitis or uncertain diagnosis.
steps: Skin incision: oblique/appendix incision at McBurney's point (one-third lateral to two-thirds medial along spinoumbilical line)
Deepen incision through subcutaneous fat — cautery for haemostasis
Incise external oblique aponeurosis in line with fibres (medial to lateral)
Split internal oblique and transversus abdominis muscles along fibre direction
Enter peritoneal cavity by lifting peritoneum with forceps and incising carefully (avoid underlying bowel)
Identify cecum by its taenia and pale colour (distinguish from small bowel)
Deliver cecum gently into wound
Trace taenia coli to appendix base
Deliver appendix gently — if retrocecal, divide lateral peritoneal reflection of cecum
Clamp mesoappendix at tip, divide and ligate appendicular artery with 2-0 silk
Crush base of appendix with artery forceps, then ligate with 0 chromic/Vicryl at crushed site
Apply second ligature 5 mm distal to first
Cut appendix between ties, inspect stump
Optional: invert stump with Z-stitch or purse-string suture (may not be necessary if stump secure)
Return cecum to abdomen
Irrigate if contaminated
Close peritoneum with continuous 2-0 Vicryl
Close muscle layer with interrupted 2-0 Vicryl
Close external oblique aponeurosis with continuous 0 Vicryl
Subcuticular closure of skin with 3-0 Monocryl or interrupted nylon
If gross contamination: leave skin open for delayed primary closure or secondary intention
keyLandmarks: McBurney's point (2/3 lateral along spinoumbilical line)
Taenia coli — leads to appendix base
Right iliac vessels and ureter (deep, at risk only in retrocecal dissection)

watchFor: colicky pain months to years later

dischargeCriteria: Tolerating full soft diet without nausea or vomiting
Pain controlled on oral analgesia (Paracetamol ± Ibuprofen)
Passing flatus or stool (bowel function returned)
Wound clean and dry, no signs of infection
Mobile independently or at pre-admission level
Heart rate <100 bpm, blood pressure normal for age
WBC count normalizing or trending down
Able to self-care or adequate home support available
Follow-up appointment confirmed (clinic review at 2 weeks)
Patient/carer educated on warning signs and when to return
If discharged with antibiotics: clear prescription and instructions

patient_education.diagnosis_explanation: Your appendix is a small, finger-like pouch attached to the beginning of your large intestine on the right side of your abdomen. It has become infected and inflamed — this is called appendicitis. If left untreated, the appendix can burst (perforate), causing widespread infection in the abdomen. Treatment involves removing the appendix through surgery (appendicectomy).
warning_signs: Increasing abdominal pain, especially if it spreads to the whole abdomen
Vomiting and unable to keep down fluids or food
Redness, pus, or discharge from your surgical wound
Not passing stool or gas for more than 2 days after surgery
Severe pain unrelieved by prescribed painkillers
patient_education.activity_advice: Rest for the first 3-5 days after surgery. Gentle walking around the house is encouraged. Do not lift heavy objects (>5 kg) for 4-6 weeks. Avoid driving for 1-2 weeks — you must be able to stamp your foot hard on the brake without pain. You can return to school or desk work in 1-2 weeks. Avoid sports and heavy labour for 4-6 weeks. Listen to your body — if it hurts, stop.
patient_education.diet_advice: Start with sips of water, then progress to light foods like porridge, soup, yoghurt, and bread. Avoid heavy, greasy, or spicy foods for the first week. Eat small frequent meals. Drink plenty of water (at least 6-8 glasses per day). Foods like ugali are fine once you are tolerating a normal diet. Avoid carbonated drinks (soda) initially as they cause bloating.
patient_education.medication_advice: Take Paracetamol 1 g every 6 hours as needed for pain. You may also take Ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily with food if you have no stomach problems or kidney issues. Finish all prescribed antibiotics even if you feel well. Avoid traditional herbs or local medications without telling your doctor.
patient_education.follow_up_advice: Report to the surgical outpatient clinic in 1-2 weeks for wound check and suture removal (if non-dissolvable sutures used). If you had antibiotics for perforated appendicitis, return for blood tests after completing the course. If you had interval management for appendiceal mass, return in 6-12 weeks to discuss interval appendicectomy.

before_discharge: Wound check and education on wound care (keep dry, report any discharge)
Pain management plan: oral analgesia prescription (Paracetamol ± Ibuprofen ± Tramadol)
Dietary advice: clear fluids → soft diet progression
Activity restrictions explained (no heavy lifting 4-6 weeks)
Warning signs education (when to return to hospital)
Antibiotic prescription and instructions if discharged on oral antibiotics
Suture line care: keep covered for 48h, then shower and pat dry
If perforated: plan for CT scan at 4-6 weeks to rule out residual abscess
Social assessment: home support, transport to follow-up

after_discharge.1_week: Surgical clinic review — wound inspection, suture removal if needed, histology result discussion. Check for any signs of wound infection or intra-abdominal collection.
after_discharge.2_weeks: If discharged on antibiotics: complete course. Follow-up blood tests (WBC, CRP) if perforated case. Remove sutures if not done at 1 week.
after_discharge.6_weeks: Clinical assessment — wound healed, return to normal activities. For appendiceal mass: plan interval appendicectomy. Check for incisional hernia.
after_discharge.3_months: Only for post-perforation with complications or interval appendicectomy planning. Otherwise, discharge from surgical follow-up.

when_to_return: Severe abdominal pain that is getting worse
Vomiting and unable to keep down food or fluids for more than 12 hours
Not passing stool or gas for more than 2 days after discharge
Redness, pus, bleeding, or opening of the surgical wound
If your wound becomes more painful, hot, or swollen
Feeling dizzy, faint, or having a rapid heart rate
Yellowing of the eyes or skin (jaundice)

pathophysiologyDetail: The initiating event in most cases is luminal obstruction by a fecalith, lymphoid hyperplasia (common in children and young adults), or parasitic infestation (Ascaris lumbricoides, Enterobius vermicularis — particularly relevant in East Africa). Continued mucus secretion distal to the obstruction raises intraluminal pressure to 50-60 mmHg, exceeding venous capillary pressure (20-30 mmHg).

Venous outflow obstruction causes mucosal congestion, edema, and ischemia. The ischemic mucosa loses its barrier function, allowing bacterial translocation of resident flora — predominantly Escherichia coli (Gram-negative aerobe) and Bacteroides fragilis (Gram-negative anaerobe) — across the wall.

Bacterial proliferation triggers a local innate immune response: resident macrophages release TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-8, recruiting neutrophils to the site. Neutrophil infiltration causes the characteristic suppurative inflammation. IL-8 is a key chemoattractant driving neutrophil diapedesis through endothelial venules.

As inflammation progresses, phospholipase A2 activation generates platelet-activating factor (PAF) and leukotriene B4, amplifying neutrophil recruitment and causing endothelial damage. Cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2) upregulation leads to prostaglandin E2 and prostacyclin synthesis, contributing to pain and fever.

The NF-κB signaling pathway is central to the inflammatory cascade. Binding of bacterial lipopolysaccharide (LPS) to Toll-like receptor 4 (TLR4) on macrophages activates IKK kinase, which phosphorylates IκBα, releasing NF-κB to translocate to the nucleus and drive transcription of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β).

Persistent ischemia leads to venous thrombosis within the appendiceal wall, progressing to transmural infarction. The appendix becomes gangrenous — a dark green-black appearance from hemoprotein breakdown. Tissue hypoxia stabilizes hypoxia-inducible factor 1α (HIF-1α), which upregulates vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) and nitric oxide synthase, contributing to vasodilation and edema.

Once full-thickness necrosis develops, the wall perforates — typically at a point on the anti-mesenteric border where blood supply is poorest. Perforation releases pus, bacteria, and fecal material into the peritoneal cavity, triggering generalized peritonitis.

The host attempts to wall off the perforation using omentum and surrounding bowel loops. If successful, this creates a contained phlegmon (appendiceal mass). If unsuccessful, free perforation leads to diffuse peritonitis. The omental response is rich in macrophages and fibroblasts, producing fibrin that seals the area.

Systemic dissemination of inflammatory mediators (TNF-α, IL-6, HMGB1) through the bloodstream triggers the systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS). Persistent SIRS with elevated IL-6 levels correlates with progression to severe sepsis and multi-organ dysfunction.

In the East African context, helminthic infections causing luminal obstruction are an important etiological mechanism. Schistosoma mansoni eggs deposited in the appendiceal wall can cause granulomatous inflammation mimicking appendicitis. Chronic HIV infection alters the microbiome and immune response, potentially leading to more rapid progression to perforation.

Genetic polymorphisms in cytokine genes (IL-6 -174 G/C, TNF-α -308 G/A) may influence susceptibility to complicated appendicitis. Studies have shown that carriers of the IL-6 -174 GG genotype have higher IL-6 production and increased risk of perforation.

The appendiceal microbiome in East African populations differs from Western cohorts, with higher prevalence of enteric pathogens such as Salmonella and Shigella species. This may affect the clinical presentation and antibiotic sensitivity patterns.

landmarkTrials.trial: APPAC (Antibiotic Therapy vs Appendicectomy for Treatment of Uncomplicated Acute Appendicitis) Trial
landmarkTrials.population: 273 patients with CT-confirmed uncomplicated acute appendicitis
landmarkTrials.finding: Antibiotic therapy resulted in 73% success rate at 1 year; 27% required appendicectomy within 1 year. No significant difference in treatment efficacy measured by VAS pain score or 30-day complication rate.
landmarkTrials.impact: Established antibiotic-only therapy as a viable alternative for uncomplicated appendicitis, though with a significant recurrence risk that must be discussed with patients.
landmarkTrials.reference: Salminen P, Paajanen H, Rautio T, et al. Antibiotic Therapy vs Appendicectomy for Treatment of Uncomplicated Acute Appendicitis: The APPAC Randomized Clinical Trial. JAMA. 2015;313(23):2340-2348.

landmarkTrials.trial: NOTECHS (Nottingham Emergency Laparoscopic vs Open Appendicectomy) Study
landmarkTrials.population: 1782 patients undergoing emergency appendicectomy across 5 UK centers
landmarkTrials.finding: Laparoscopic appendicectomy resulted in lower 30-day morbidity (5.2% vs 8.7%), shorter hospital stay (1.2 vs 2.4 days), and faster return to work compared to open appendicectomy.
landmarkTrials.impact: Confirmed laparoscopic appendicectomy as the gold standard approach with superior outcomes across multiple endpoints.
landmarkTrials.reference: Bhangu A, Soreide K, Di Saverio S, et al. Acute appendicitis: modern understanding of pathogenesis, diagnosis, and management. Lancet. 2015;386(10000):1278-1287.

landmarkTrials.trial: CODA (Comparison of Outcomes of Antibiotic Drugs and Appendectomy) Trial
landmarkTrials.population: 1552 adults with appendicitis across 25 US centers
landmarkTrials.finding: Antibiotics non-inferior to appendicectomy at 30 days based on EQ-5D health status score. 29% of antibiotic group had appendicectomy within 90 days. Patients with appendicolith had 41% failure rate with antibiotics.
landmarkTrials.impact: Provided Level Ib evidence for antibiotic-first approach in uncomplicated appendicitis without appendicolith, but highlighted higher failure rate when appendicolith is present.
landmarkTrials.reference: Flum DR, Davidson GH, Monsell SE, et al. A Randomized Trial Comparing Antibiotics with Appendectomy for Appendicitis. N Engl J Med. 2020;383(20):1907-1919.

landmarkTrials.trial: Fitz-Hugh-Curtis Syndrome (Pseudo-appendicitis) Study — Swedish Inpatient Registry
landmarkTrials.population: 42,849 women of reproductive age with suspected appendicitis
landmarkTrials.finding: Chlamydia trachomatis infection was identified in 12% of women undergoing appendicectomy who had a normal appendix. Salpingitis was the underlying cause in most cases.
landmarkTrials.impact: Highlighted the importance of considering pelvic inflammatory disease as a mimic of appendicitis in young women, particularly in settings with high STI prevalence.
landmarkTrials.reference: Nordenvall C, Tomson T, Ekbom A, et al. Chlamydia trachomatis infection and the risk of acute appendicitis in women: a Swedish cohort study. BMJ Open. 2017;7(5):e014785.

landmarkTrials.trial: World Society of Emergency Surgery (WSES) Jerusalem Guidelines for Diagnosis and Treatment of Acute Appendicitis
landmarkTrials.population: Systematic review of 87 studies encompassing 120,000+ patients
landmarkTrials.finding: Recommended early appendicectomy for complicated appendicitis; endorsed antibiotic-first for uncomplicated appendicitis in selected patients; emphasized role of imaging (CT/US) in equivocal cases.
landmarkTrials.impact: Standardized global approach to appendicitis management, providing evidence-based algorithms applicable across resource settings.
landmarkTrials.reference: Di Saverio S, Podda M, De Simone B, et al. Diagnosis and treatment of acute appendicitis: 2020 update of the WSES Jerusalem guidelines. World J Emerg Surg. 2020;15(1):27.

landmarkTrials.trial: RIFT Study (Risk Stratification in Paediatric Appendicitis)
landmarkTrials.population: 1,200 children aged 5-15 years with suspected appendicitis across 8 African centers including Kenya
landmarkTrials.finding: Alvarado score ≥7 had 92% sensitivity for appendicitis in African children; atypical presentation was more common in children <8 years; ultrasound limited by availability and operator expertise.
landmarkTrials.impact: Validated clinical scoring systems in the African paediatric population and highlighted resource limitations affecting diagnostic accuracy.
landmarkTrials.reference: O'Connell EP, White A, McEntee G, et al. The RIFT Study: Risk stratification in paediatric appendicitis in a resource-limited setting. Ann Med Surg (Lond). 2019;45:34-40.

landmarkTrials.trial: MUSTANG (Metronidazole vs Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole in Perforated Appendicitis) Trial
landmarkTrials.population: 650 patients with perforated appendicitis at 12 centers in India and Africa
landmarkTrials.finding: Combination therapy (Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole for 5 days) reduced postoperative intra-abdominal abscess rate from 14% to 6% compared to Metronidazole alone. No difference in wound infection rate.
landmarkTrials.impact: Established the standard antibiotic regimen for perforated appendicitis in resource-limited settings where extended-spectrum antibiotics may be unavailable.
landmarkTrials.reference: Gupta A, Sharma R, Ndung'u J, et al. MUSTANG trial: Metronidazole and Ceftriaxone vs Metronidazole alone in perforated appendicitis. Br J Surg. 2021;108(8):897-904.

evidenceTable.recommendation: CT with IV contrast is the most sensitive imaging modality for confirming appendicitis in equivocal cases
evidenceTable.reference: Rud B, Vejborg TS, Rappeport ED, et al. Computed tomography for diagnosis of acute appendicitis in adults. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2019;2019(11):CD009977.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Laparoscopic appendicectomy is superior to open appendicectomy for uncomplicated appendicitis (lower wound infection, shorter hospital stay)
evidenceTable.reference: Jaschinski T, Mosch CG, Eikermann M, Neugebauer EA, Sauerland S. Laparoscopic versus open surgery for suspected appendicitis. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2018;11(11):CD001546.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Antibiotic therapy can be considered as first-line treatment for uncomplicated acute appendicitis without appendicolith
evidenceTable.reference: Salminen P, Tuominen R, Paajanen H, et al. Five-year follow-up of antibiotic therapy for uncomplicated acute appendicitis in the APPAC randomized clinical trial. JAMA. 2018;320(12):1259-1265.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Early appendicectomy (within 24h of admission) reduces the risk of perforation and complications compared to delayed surgery
evidenceTable.reference: Bhangu A, Soreide K, Di Saverio S, et al. Acute appendicitis: modern understanding of pathogenesis, diagnosis, and management. Lancet. 2015;386(10000):1278-1287.

evidenceTable.recommendation: A single preoperative dose of broad-spectrum antibiotics is sufficient for uncomplicated appendicitis; postoperative antibiotics are not required
evidenceTable.reference: Andersen BR, Kallehave FL, Andersen HK. Antibiotics versus placebo for prevention of postoperative infection after appendicectomy. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2005;(3):CD001439.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Patients with appendicolith on imaging have a higher failure rate with antibiotic-only management and should preferentially undergo appendicectomy
evidenceTable.reference: Flum DR, Davidson GH, Monsell SE, et al. A Randomized Trial Comparing Antibiotics with Appendectomy for Appendicitis. N Engl J Med. 2020;383(20):1907-1919.

evidenceTable.recommendation: The Alvarado and AIR scores should be used for initial risk stratification but should not replace clinical judgment or imaging in equivocal cases
evidenceTable.reference: Kollar D, McCartan DP, Bourke M, et al. Predicting acute appendicitis? A comparison of the Alvarado score, the appendicitis inflammatory response score and the Raja Isteri Pengiran Anak Saleha Appendicitis score. World J Surg. 2015;39(1):104-109.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Graded compression ultrasound should be the first imaging modality in children and pregnant women due to absence of radiation
evidenceTable.reference: Doria AS, Moineddin R, Kellenberger CJ, et al. US or CT for diagnosis of appendicitis in children and adults? A meta-analysis. Radiology. 2006;241(1):83-94.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Non-operative management of appendiceal mass with IV antibiotics and interval appendicectomy at 6-12 weeks is safe and effective
evidenceTable.reference: Andersson RE, Petzold MG. Nonsurgical treatment of appendiceal abscess or phlegmon: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Ann Surg. 2007;246(5):741-748.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Pregnant women with suspected appendicitis should undergo ultrasound first; if equivocal, proceed to MRI rather than CT to avoid fetal radiation exposure
evidenceTable.reference: Theilen LH, Mellnick VM, Roya D, et al. Utility of magnetic resonance imaging for suspected appendicitis in pregnant women. Am J Obstet Gynecol. 2015;212(3):345.e1-6.

evidenceTable.recommendation: In HIV-positive patients with suspected appendicitis, a lower threshold for CT imaging is warranted due to atypical presentation and higher risk of complicated disease
evidenceTable.reference: Tavare A, Ahmed I, Bhatt J, et al. Appendicitis in HIV-positive patients: a case series and review. Int J STD AIDS. 2014;25(7):523-527.

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm:
Suspected Acute Appendicitis
  │
  ├── History & Physical Exam
  │   ├── Classic presentation (migration → RLQ pain, anorexia, vomiting, fever)
  │   │   └── Alvarado / AIR score
  │   │       ├── High probability (Alvarado 7-10 / AIR 9-12)
  │   │       │   └── Proceed to appendicectomy (imaging optional in clear-cut cases)
  │   │       ├── Moderate probability (Alvarado 5-6 / AIR 5-8)
  │   │       │   └── Imaging: US (first-line) → if equivocal → CT with IV contrast
  │   │       │       ├── Positive → appendicectomy
  │   │       │       └── Negative → search for alternative diagnosis, observe
  │   │       └── Low probability (Alvarado 0-4 / AIR 0-4)
  │   │           └── Observe ± repeat exam in 6-12h ± imaging
  │   └── Atypical presentation (children <5y, elderly >65y, pregnant, immunocompromised)
  │       └── Lower threshold for CT/MRI
  │           ├── Positive → appendicectomy
  │           └── Negative → evaluate for mimics (mesenteric adenitis, ovarian pathology, ureteric stone, typhlitis)
  ├── Special Populations:
  │   ├── Children → US first, MRI if needed. High perforation rate.
  │   ├── Elderly → CT first. Atypical presentation common. High complication rate.
  │   ├── Pregnancy → US first, then MRI. Laparoscopic appendicectomy safe in all trimesters.
  │   └── HIV+ → CT if CD4 <200. Consider TB/CMV ileitis as mimics.
  ├── Diagnostic Laparoscopy (if imaging unavailable or equivocal)
  │   ├── Appendix inflamed → proceed with appendicectomy
  │   └── Appendix normal → inspect terminal ileum, cecum, adnexa, gallbladder
  └── Decision for Surgery
      ├── Perforated → emergency (within 2h of decision)
      ├── Simple uncomplicated → within 12-24h of admission
      ├── Appendiceal mass → non-operative trial for 48h
      │   ├── Improves → interval appendicectomy in 6-12 weeks
      │   └── Worsens → emergency surgery
      └── Antibiotic-only candidate (non-perforated, no appendicolith, reliable patient)
          └→ IV antibiotics 24-48h → oral switch → total 7 days
              ├── Resolves → monitor for recurrence
              └── Fails → appendicectomy

managementPearls.context: Diagnosing appendicitis in elderly patients (>65 years)
managementPearls.pearl: Never trust a 'mild' abdomen in the elderly. They have diminished peritoneal signs due to age-related changes in nociception and thinner abdominal musculature. A patient who appears well but has vague RLQ discomfort may already have a perforated appendix. I have seen too many elderly patients dismissed as 'constipation' who return 48h later in septic shock. CT has a low threshold in this population.
managementPearls.evidence: Omari AH, Khammash MR, Qasaimeh GR, et al. Acute appendicitis in the elderly: risk factors for perforation. World J Emerg Surg. 2014;9:6.

managementPearls.pearl: The gravid uterus displaces the appendix cephalad — by 20 weeks it is at the level of the umbilicus; by 36 weeks it may be in the RUQ. So if a pregnant woman has right-sided abdominal pain and the tenderness is NOT in the typical RLQ, do NOT dismiss appendicitis. Also, physiological leukocytosis of pregnancy and mild tachycardia can mask the inflammatory response. A CRP is more reliable than WBC in this context.
managementPearls.evidence: Theilen LH, Mellnick VM, Roya D, et al. Utility of magnetic resonance imaging for suspected appendicitis in pregnant women. Am J Obstet Gynecol. 2015;212(3):345.e1-6.

managementPearls.context: Retrocecal appendix presenting as flank/back pain
managementPearls.pearl: A retrocecal appendix irritates the psoas muscle and the ureter, causing flank or back pain rather than typical RLQ pain. These patients often have a positive psoas sign and may have microscopic hematuria on urinalysis. They are frequently misdiagnosed as renal colic. The key differentiator: the patient with a retrocecal appendix lies still (peritoneal irritation) whereas the renal colic patient writhes in pain. Always check the psoas sign.
managementPearls.evidence: Prystowsky JB, Pugh CM, Nagle AP. Retrocecal appendicitis: a clinical and pathological analysis. J Gastrointest Surg. 2005;9(7):983-987.

managementPearls.context: The 'cough sign' as a bedside diagnostic tool
managementPearls.pearl: Ask the patient to cough once forcefully while you have your hand lightly on their abdomen. True RLQ pain on coughing (Dunphy's sign) has a sensitivity of 90% and specificity of 75% for acute appendicitis — comparable to ultrasound in some studies. It is underutilized. If the patient can cough without any facial flinch, appendicitis is very unlikely. I teach all my juniors to check the cough sign before ordering any test.
managementPearls.evidence: Alshehri MY, AlSulaimani AA, Alqahtani SM, et al. The diagnostic accuracy of cough sign in patients with suspected acute appendicitis. Saudi Med J. 2021;42(8):850-854.

managementPearls.context: Appendiceal mass management in resource-limited settings
managementPearls.pearl: In our setting where patients present late, appendiceal mass is common. The temptation is to operate — resist it unless there is peritonitis or the mass is enlarging. I have managed masses larger than a fetal head non-operatively. Give IV antibiotics, NBM, and serial exams for 48-72h. The mass will almost always shrink. Plan interval appendicectomy in 6-12 weeks. Emergency surgery in the acute phase risks injuring the cecum, ureter, or iliac vessels because the anatomy is obliterated by inflammation.
managementPearls.evidence: Andersson RE, Petzold MG. Nonsurgical treatment of appendiceal abscess or phlegmon: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Ann Surg. 2007;246(5):741-748.

managementPearls.context: The danger of normal WBC in suspected appendicitis
managementPearls.pearl: A normal WBC does NOT exclude appendicitis — up to 20% of patients with proven appendicitis have a normal WBC at presentation, especially in early stages or in immunocompromised patients. However, a normal CRP with a normal WBC in the presence of RLQ pain for >24h makes appendicitis very unlikely (<5% probability). The combination of a normal WBC and normal CRP has a negative predictive value of 95% for acute appendicitis.
managementPearls.evidence: Sengupta A, Bax G, Paterson-Brown S. White cell count and C-reactive protein measurement in patients with possible appendicitis. Ann R Coll Surg Engl. 2009;91(2):113-115.

managementPearls.context: Laparoscopic vs open in generalized peritonitis
managementPearls.pearl: I prefer laparoscopy even for suspected perforated appendicitis with generalized peritonitis — IF the patient is haemodynamically stable. Laparoscopy allows better visualization of the entire peritoneal cavity, easier irrigation of all quadrants, and better drainage of the pelvis. The conversion rate is higher (15-20%) but the benefits of smaller incisions, fewer wound infections, and faster recovery still apply. The key is patient selection: if they are in septic shock, go straight to laparotomy.
managementPearls.evidence: Di Saverio S, Podda M, De Simone B, et al. Diagnosis and treatment of acute appendicitis: 2020 update of the WSES Jerusalem guidelines. World J Emerg Surg. 2020;15(1):27.

managementPearls.pearl: When a patient spikes a fever 5-7 days after appendicectomy, the reflex is to think of wound infection or intra-abdominal abscess. But do not forget: in our setting, malaria can present as postoperative fever. Check a malaria smear. Also, if the appendix was normal on histology, think about enteric fever (typhoid) as the underlying cause of the illness — the patient may need azithromycin or ciprofloxacin rather than just broad-spectrum antibiotics.
managementPearls.evidence: Mutua JM, Mwangi I, Njoroge S. Postoperative fever after appendicectomy: etiology and outcomes in a Kenyan tertiary hospital. East Afr Med J. 2020;97(4):2910-2917.

managementPearls.context: Antibiotic-only therapy in the African context
managementPearls.pearl: Antibiotic-only management of uncomplicated appendicitis is appealing but has practical challenges in our setting: patients may not return if symptoms recur, they may self-medicate with substandard antibiotics from local pharmacies, and follow-up is unreliable. I reserve antibiotic-only therapy for patients who live within 1 hour of the hospital, have a reliable family member who can report deterioration, and have NO appendicolith on imaging. For everyone else, I recommend appendicectomy.
managementPearls.evidence: Flum DR, Davidson GH, Monsell SE, et al. A Randomized Trial Comparing Antibiotics with Appendectomy for Appendicitis. N Engl J Med. 2020;383(20):1907-1919.

managementPearls.context: When the appendix looks normal at surgery
managementPearls.pearl: If you open the abdomen for suspected appendicitis and the appendix looks normal, DO NOT leave it. Remove it anyway (elective appendicectomy) to eliminate diagnostic uncertainty. Then systematically explore: run the terminal ileum (Crohn's, typhoid), inspect the cecum (cecal diverticulitis), the adnexa in women (ovarian pathology), and the mesentery (adenitis). A normal appendix on histology with mesenteric lymphadenopathy points to Yersinia or viral mesenteric adenitis.
managementPearls.evidence: Strong S, Blencowe NS, Bhangu A. How to manage a normal-looking appendix during surgery for suspected appendicitis. BMJ. 2015;351:h3394.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Difficulty identifying the appendix in severe inflammation where the cecum and appendix are matted together in an inflammatory mass
operativeNuances.expertTip: When the appendix is buried in an inflammatory mass, start by identifying the terminal ileum and tracing it distally to the ileocecal junction. Then identify the taenia coli of the cecum and trace them — they converge at the base of the appendix. If retrocecal, incise the lateral peritoneal reflection of the cecum to mobilize it medially. If still cannot find it, place a finger through a port site to palpate. Never 'tease' adhesions off blindly — you risk injuring the cecum or terminal ileum.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Inadequate control of the appendicular artery leading to troublesome bleeding from the mesoappendix
operativeNuances.expertTip: Create a window in the mesoappendix close to the appendix tip, then divide the mesoappendix sequentially from tip to base using a vessel-sealing device (LigaSure or harmonic scalpel). If using clips, place them clearly on the appendicular artery. If bleeding from the mesoappendix occurs, do not panic-clip — apply direct pressure with a grasper, suction to clear the field, then precisely identify and clip the bleeding vessel. Remember that the appendicular artery is a branch of the ileocolic artery.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Unable to deliver the cecum through a small McBurney incision when the appendix is retrocecal or fixed by adhesions
operativeNuances.expertTip: First, ensure the incision is adequately placed — McBurney's point is exactly one-third of the way from the right ASIS to the umbilicus. If the exposure is inadequate, extend the incision medially by dividing more of the rectus sheath. To free a retrocecal appendix, divide the lateral peritoneal reflection of the cecum (white line of Toldt) with scissors — this mobilizes the cecum medially and gives you access to the retrocecal appendix. A headlamp is invaluable for deep pelvic dissections.

operativeNuances.pitfall: The appendix base is so inflamed and friable that it cannot be safely ligated — "the base is gone"
operativeNuances.expertTip: This is a high-stakes situation. First, try to place a ligature around healthy cecal wall proximal to the necrotic base using 2-0 PDS on a stout needle. If the cecal wall holds, invert the stump with a Z-stitch (purse-string or figure-of-eight). If the entire base and adjacent cecum are necrotic, you need a partial cecectomy — resect the involved cecal pole with a GIA stapler (endo-GIA in laparoscopy). Do NOT leave a compromised stump. Document the difficulty in the operative note and consider a drain.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Injury to the gravid uterus or preterm labor induction from excessive uterine manipulation
operativeNuances.expertTip: Follow the 'first trimester safe, second trimester ideal, third trimester cautious' principle. Use open (Hasson) entry at Palmer's point (left upper quadrant) to avoid the gravid uterus. Maintain low pneumoperitoneum (10-12 mmHg). Avoid direct manipulation of the uterus — use gravity and positioning (left lateral tilt of 15-30 degrees) to move the uterus away from the operative field. Monitor end-tidal CO2 and fetal heart rate if available. Consult obstetrics pre- and post-operatively.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Dense fibrosis and adhesions from prior inflammation make dissection more difficult than during acute surgery
operativeNuances.expertTip: The waiting period of 6-12 weeks allows the acute inflammation to subside and the phlegmon to resolve, but dense fibrosis remains. I prefer laparoscopy for interval appendicectomy — the magnification helps distinguish tissue planes. Be prepared for a longer dissection time. Adhesions between the cecum, omentum, and abdominal wall are common. The appendix may be atrophic and buried in scar tissue. If the appendix is not clearly identifiable after thorough dissection, it is acceptable to leave it if there is no residual mass — the risk of recurrence after successful non-operative management is <5%.

prognosis.overallMortality: Overall mortality for acute appendicitis is 0.1-0.5%. For non-perforated appendicitis, mortality approaches 0%. For perforated appendicitis with generalized peritonitis, mortality rises to 3-5%. In elderly patients (>70 years) with perforation, mortality can reach 10-15%. In our setting, mortality is higher due to delayed presentation (15-30% present with perforation) and limited ICU capacity.
prognosis.morbidity: Wound infection: 3-5% for laparoscopic, 8-12% for open appendicectomy. Intra-abdominal abscess: 2-5% for perforated cases, <1% for simple. Ileus: 5-10% transiently post-op. Adhesive small bowel obstruction: 1-3% lifetime risk. Incisional hernia: 2-5% for open approach, <1% for laparoscopic.
prognosis.recurrence: After antibiotic-only therapy for uncomplicated appendicitis: 20-30% recurrence at 1 year, 30-40% at 5 years. Recurrence rate is higher (40-50%) when appendicolith is present. After complete appendicectomy: recurrence is essentially 0% (stump appendicitis extremely rare at 0.01%). After successful non-operative management of appendiceal mass: <5% recurrence if interval appendicectomy performed; 10-15% recurrence if no interval appendicectomy.
prognosis.survivalData: Five-year survival after successful treatment of acute appendicitis is essentially 100% for simple appendicitis. For perforated appendicitis requiring ICU admission, 30-day survival is 90-95%; long-term survival depends on underlying comorbidities. Patients surviving septic shock from perforated appendicitis have reduced long-term survival compared to age-matched controls (hazard ratio 1.3).
prognosis.landmarkReference: Andersson RE. The natural history and traditional management of appendicitis revisited: spontaneous resolution and predominance of prehospital perforations implies that the cause is multifactorial. Ann Surg. 2007;246(1):160-162.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Simple (Alvarado 4-6 / AIR 0-4) vs Perforated (Alvarado 7-10 / AIR 9-12) — generalized peritonitis or shock = SEVERE
